body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

transcript.text: oh hi according to the National research Council of Canada's official Canadian time it is now 6:30 pm welcome to the Ohio working group I'm Richard barn on the screen you can see uh my co-chair Siobhan sahib and I think Siobhan will uh kick us off with a few administr thanks Richard uh welcome all you have probably seen the slide several times uh during this week but it is really important so please do note it well it covers several topics such as patents and code of conduct um and please do feel free um to reach out to amed's team if um you experience behavior that you're uncomfortable with uh and it the note will also cover several BCPS and documents that um describe part ipation in the ITF just some meeting tips um the important one here is that please use meet Echo to join the mic que so that we can um keep things Fair between remote participants and um in person participants and um if you're not using the on-site tool please do record your participation using the QR code that's probably up there somewhere in the room and if you're joining remotely should al already be taken care of um and yeah make sure your audio and video are off unless you're presenting if you're a remote participant and use a headset we have some note takers thanks to Laura and Chris for helping take notes and yeah we can begin um Tommy will be talking about deployment experience with octp which will lead into the chunk OHP draft discussion which is our adopted draft for now and just quickly check before we launch into it any uh proposed changes to this agenda are we all good with this sounds good all right Tommy take it away okay maybe even a be quick so we can let people get to their dinners or whatever else lies on the other side of ohigh all right great I'm Tommy Paulie from Apple um and so first I wanted to share some background on the uh experience that we've been having with a couple different use cases for oblivious HTTP uh just to see kind of how they're set up how they're configured how they perform um and uh I specifically asked to actually do this before our discussion on chunk OHP because some of the uh details of how we actually deploy these things and test these things and what we've run into are relevant to what we think we should be doing in that draft okay so I'm just going to first go over three different use cases uh where we are employing oblivious HTTP and then talk generically about uh how we're doing a configuration for OHP and how that's being shared all right so the first one that we've been doing for the longest period of time is for doing safe browsing type checks um and this is this is like an example of like iOS UI for what it would look like in Safari um so this is a a protocol that previously already existed you have cases where you have uh a big database of truncated URL hashes that indicate buckets of things that may be fishing or malware or some bad type of and uh so those are downloaded periodically and then if a client has a hit on one of these truncated hashes uh they go and fetch the complete bucket of complete hashes and figure out if the site they're going to is potentially malicious and then they will show this thing and it's very important to have privacy on these checks because uh when you are well a you're fetching these things in the background quite a lot and so this would allow the server to just kind like watch where everyone goes uh but also when you actually do a look up here for something that is potentially on the list that is revealing some information about what you may be accessing so it's very important to have good privacy here so the overall setup that uh we're doing uh so we have on on the left I have you know two clients just represent there's a multitude of different clients and they are communicating generally over htb3 by default it can fall back to http2 if uh UDP is blocked on your network but generally it's hb3 and these are somewhat longlived connections uh in the background uh from the browser client going to the relay which is operated by a third- party partner and then there's a uh pretty pretty long lived connection or a set of long lived hb2 connections that are uh mutually authenticated with TLS between the relay and the O Gateway which is uh also where the uh safe Rising service Target is and then the clients are authenticating to the relay using a privacy pass uh token so the uh clients need uh to kind of be able to fetch that ahead of time just to say they're legitimate clients that are expected to use the service and for this case there's no actual uh authentication to the back end there uh and as I mentioned before there's like a periodic update uh that happens in the background and then some more uh real time updates when you have a potential hit so just some of the properties here these are normally because they're in the background and periodic it's normally a cold start connection so like these fetches are not reusing connections that much they're just kind of creating a new connection to the relay it's predominantly in the background most of the time this latency is not actually blocking users uh I think this the frequency we see for people actually having a potential hit is like once a day so normally this is just like a background thing and when we look at the times for downloading that whole database globally over OHP we're seeing it be like on average like 350 milliseconds for the whole thing which is okay it's a background thing so it's not super important to optimize further than that so that's one example of omtp here there's another case uh which was actually discussed at the last ITF um I believe when we were talking about some of the cases where we could have this like unreliable OHP um this is for like dap metrics upload um in this case you're just trying to essentially post an upload of things to the server the responses are not used in any way and so this is a case that could be optimized U by allowing the client to just hand the message off to the relay and then not care if it actually got all the way to the gate where it was accepted they just want to shove the metrics up in this case uh there actually are two sets of privacy passes going on here um but otherwise it's a very similar setup and it's like on the order of a day or a couple times a day that this is happening and then the newer case that we have uh that we're using is uh for model inference uh so uh recently uh we announced some private Cloud compute uh features for various generative AI things there's a Blog you can read about all the details there and it explains that it is using oblivious HP um and this is a case where you have potentially large responses or small responses and they uh take a varying amount of time to generate and there's a lot of value to having the beginning of a response and then being able to trickle in the rest of the large uh generated response over time uh but again it's a very similar setup you have many clients with uh reused and longer lived hp3 connections they're using a privacy pass to the relay there's also a separate privacy pass that's being used all the way to that inference server there's a mutually authenticated http2 Connection in between and this is uh more real- time based on user activity when a user would be using some of these features Tommy quick clarifying question the the Gateway privacy pass here in a minute I think the prior example is that uh held by the relay or does that kind of pass through through the it's it's an end to end so it's within the encrypted hpk package that is decrypted by the server yeah that's right so it's saying that uh this client has access to that it's also a way to uh provide some rate limiting where if you limit how many Gateway privacy passes the client can do that can kind of controls how many uh AI inference operations they can do on that model so this one is more different it uh uses chunked OHP and that has uh you know new features that that comes along with and in this case uh previously we had been doing the other features and we didn't have to implement things like indeterminate length binary HTTP we didn't have to have a binary HTP trailer support because if you're doing single messages that cannot be decrypted partially there's no value for trailer Fields uh since you have your header Fields always in the same bucket um and so the these were things that in deployment experience and implementation experience uh until you start doing chunk uh you really aren't really exercising what it means to do indeterminate length binary HP and to have some of the other features like trailer parsing Etc so just uh heads up of things to test um and one of the other interesting things we'll talk about later in the chunk OHP draft is that when we were testing this through different uh CDN provided relays that were doing the chunk HP relaying um we found it very common that by default with post they were doing buffering of the entire message uh and waiting for the end of the message before they would send any bites up to the server or back um and that is uh kind of defeats the purpose of doing chunk and we're like well this this is not giving us the performance we want and it's like having some really huge delays in these cases and they're all relatively easy to fix but is something that is uh also not obvious if you're just doing the normal uh oblivious HTP with a post that's all in one go so those are the three different use cases wanted to share um as far as how we do the configuration and how we do that deployment we have a configuration bag for kind of all of the oblivious HTTP servers that we know about and their Associated relays and we fetch that every like 12 to 24 hours uh to get updates there and this bag includes uh the different op configs and each of those has the Gateway key configuration and that's the thing that is in the normal OHP draft or the RFC it uh also then says here's the relay that you need to go through and like the path on the relay for the Relay resource um it indicates kind of like what are the supported targets that I can get to behind this Gateway so I can know how to route them and then it also indicates whether or not you are supposed to be using chunked or non- chunked uh for this particular Rule and uh if you look into some of the stuff we're doing for the private Cloud compute features there's a lot in there around trying to make sure that there's non- targetability and so these configs are also being um replicated into a transparency log and we're validating against that and so that's another aspect of this config so this is uh essentially one model of how you can get the information about HTTP um and it's you know definitely kind of like the driven you know proprietary config that you like the client is knows about and is fetching but it is a way uh that does work and it be curious to hear if other people have experience with other mechanisms and if they are similar or different to this and that's all I have you any questions any questions comments yeah I had a question Tommy yeah um if you go to the previous slide you mentioned the transparency log um can you say more about this um so I mean this is essentially like alongside the op configuration there's uh like some proofs that uh you can uh check in a transparency log that is all detailed on like the private Cloud compute stuff um I uh that will do a better job of explaining some of the details than I can um but yeah it's essentially just like a blob that you then verify is in the transparency it's it would be similar to like a key transparency type solution it's just a bigger bundle of things yes I'm just wondering if you think this is something worth generalizing and um because this question comes up um during OHP discussions um do you feel like this would something that could be used Beyond or could be in a draft or something yeah I think it could um I think it' be worth uh exploring and also kind of hearing from other people who may be trying to use OHP if this is an approach that they are looking at taking or expect to take because I know we've had discussions about you know can you do transparency to uh provide the consistency or do you do other things like a double check um and so in this case uh doing the transparency log was what we went for for this architecture but I I could see other more Dynamic cases being ones that uh double check would be easier on all right other questions or comments Le to the mic thank you for this all right now thec unusually unusually large amount of information from Apple [Music] okay uh so now we'll be talking about the chunked olous HTTP messages draft which is on 01 so just as a recap and we kind of mentioned it in the last presentation chunk HTP is about allowing you to encrypt and decrypt the request and responses for OHP in separate chunks and so the layout of it is you have the existing kind of request header um that you have and then on the responses the header is just Aon but instead of one hpk or a aead encrypted uh response you have a little length field that goes before each chunk and you have multiple chunks it's a very uh straightforward idea but you know it has as with everything many complications in the details and as I mentioned before this allows use of indeterminate mode of binary HTTP it uses the built-in support from hpk for having multiple messages and conceptually is still just a single HTTP request and response it's not a unlimited streaming bidirectional thing it's just a way to not have to do the entire message all in one go yeah so things that were changed in the 01 version uh we fixed one issue which was an easy one so we did it um around how many chunks you could have uh hpk already does this correctly by having a Max number but the response is also needed to specify the same thing uh your counter should not um exceed the values that you could store in the knots for uniqueness um since that would cause knots reuse so don't do that uh there are some other to-dos that we have of doing the test vectors and some of the protocol formal analysis that just need to get done when we have time which we don't have uh but the most uh interesting issue that has had discussion that I wanted to highlight today is the one that I was hinting at for the deployment experience where you have incremental forwarding issues um on the various CDN that are handling the post messages for oblivious HTP so our deployment testing found that most of the common implementations uh did buffering for post and would wait until you had completed your entire request or wait until you got your entire response before forwarding um on any of the bytes so that uh as I mentioned before does render the chunk mode pretty ineffective and uh so we have good discussion on the issue kuho put pointed out a lot of great things in the discussion and we were going back and forth so one of the references that is useful here is from RFC 9110 on uh hqp semantics and it points out that um while the HP message can be processed a stream for incremental processing the senders and recipients cannot rely on that incremental delivery of partial messages uh since the implementations can buffer or delay the message forwarding so that's exactly what we're hitting um it is up to implementations to do what they want to do it is allowable uh it's all equally allowable to have incremental processing as well as buffering so there are a couple different options for what to do here uh one option doesn't involve anything much about uh the actual spec other than maybe giving some advice but you could essentially just say do what we did so far and fix the servers um that are doing this like if you know you're going to be used as a chunk omtp relay you should not be buffering those post messages um so simply like those inter intermediators that are being used for this get updated we could just give some advice in the document and say if you want this to be effective don't buffer and if you have a deployment that is buffering you just ask them to stop doing that U for the cases where as what we've seen so far in this deployment experience there's a pretty uh concrete setup where a client is getting a bag of like hereo the known relays that you should use in this mode and here are the gateways they work with and they're all authenticated with token or Mutual TLS uh this works okay it's not super elegant but it can work the other second approach would be like a much bigger design fix uh one of the things that was brought up on the issue is like well you know post is much more common to have buffering but there are things like connect that obviously don't do the buffering and so you could like switch uh HTTP um or at least this mode of omtp to stop using post to use extend Ed connect instead uh but that really changes the protocol quite a lot more um it loses a lot of the advantages that you have of just saying I want to use you
